name: Log Analysis & SIEM
overview:
Log analysis is the backbone of detection and incident response. Every attacker leaves traces in logs — authentication events, process creation, service installations, firewall changes, and PowerShell execution. In this learning path, you'll progress from parsing individual log files to correlating artifacts across multiple sources to reconstruct full attack timelines.

You'll work with Windows Event Logs (Security, Sysmon, PowerShell, Defender), Linux auth logs, web server access logs, Suricata IDS alerts, and cloud audit trails. These are the same skills used daily by SOC analysts, detection engineers, and incident responders.
prerequisites: Basic understanding of Windows and Linux operating systems
Familiarity with command-line tools (grep, jq, PowerShell basics)
Understanding of Active Directory concepts (domains, authentication, Kerberos)

coreTools.name: EvtxECmd (Eric Zimmerman)
coreTools.description: Command-line tool for parsing Windows Event Log (.evtx) files into CSV/JSON for analysis

installCommands.linux: yay -S dotnet-runtime && dotnet tool install --global EZTools

coreTools.name: Chainsaw
coreTools.description: Fast Windows event log hunting tool with Sigma rule support — built in Rust for speed

coreTools.name: Timeline Explorer
coreTools.description: GUI spreadsheet tool optimized for large forensic CSV files — essential companion for EvtxECmd output

installCommands.linux: Windows-only — use a Windows VM or Wine

coreTools.name: jq
coreTools.description: Command-line JSON processor — essential for parsing Suricata alerts, CloudTrail logs, and JSON-formatted event data

entryRecommendation: Start with **Brutus** if you're new to log analysis — it covers Linux `auth.log` parsing with basic grep/cut commands. If you're already comfortable with Linux logs and want to jump into Windows, start at **Unit42** for Sysmon log analysis. If you're confident with individual log sources and want multi-artifact correlation, skip to **Logjammer**.
